analogy: Think of "Human Resources" through the chapter's own opening images: Social and Political Life 74 Chapter 6 Confronting Marginalisation In the previous chapter, we read about two different groups and their experiences of inequality and discrimination. Though powerless, such groups have fought, protested and struggled against being excluded or dominated by others. They have attempted... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 77 Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation, GLOSSARY, Human Resources, Human. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Human Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that social and Political Life 74 Chapter 6 Confronting Marginalisation In the previous chapter, we read about two different groups and their experiences of inequality and discrimination.

The text explains that though powerless, such groups have fought, protested and struggled against being excluded or dominated by others.

The text explains that they have attempted to overcome their situation by adopting a range of strategies in their long history.

The text explains that religious solace, armed struggle, self improvement and education, economic uplift – there appears to be no one way of doing things.

The text explains that in all cases, the choice of struggle has depended on the circumstances that the marginalised find themselves in.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will read about some of the ways in which groups and individuals challenge existing inequalities.

The text explains that adivasis, Dalits, Muslims, women and other marginal groups argue that simply by being citizens of a democratic country, they possess equal rights that must be respected.

The text explains that many among them look up to the Constitution to address their concerns.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will see why the Constitution of India is something that marginalised groups invoke in the course of their struggles.

The text explains that as part of this, we will look at how rights are translated into laws to protect groups from continued exploitation and we will also look at the government’s efforts to formulate policies to promote the access of these groups to development.

The text explains that 75 Confronting Marginalisation The Constitution, as you have learnt in the first chapter of this book, lays down the principles that make our society and polity democratic.

The text explains that they are defined in and through the list of Fundamental Rights that are an important part of the Constitution.

The text explains that these rights are available to all Indians equally.

The text explains that as far as the marginalised are concerned, they have drawn on these rights in two ways: first, by insisting on their Fundamental Rights, they have forced the government to recognise the injustice done to them.

The text explains that second, they have insisted that the government enforce these laws.

The text explains that in some instances, the struggles of the marginalised have influenced the government to frame new laws, in keeping with the spirit of the Fundamental Rights.

The text explains that article 17 of the Constitution states that untouchability has been abolished – what this means is that no one can henceforth prevent Dalits from educating themselves, entering temples, using public facilities etc.

The text explains that it also means that it is wrong to practise untouchability and that this practice will not be tolerated by a democratic government.

The text explains that there are other sections in the Constitution that help to strengthen the argument against untouchability – for example, Article 15 of the Constitution notes that no citizen of India shall be discriminated against on the basis of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth (you learnt a lot about this in your Class VII textbook in the chapter On Equality in Indian Democracy).

The text explains that invoking Fundamental Rights Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation 2024-25

The text explains that 99 Soyrabai is questioning the idea of purity and arguing that since every human is born in the same manner, there is nothing that makes one body less or more pure than the other.

The text explains that she is possibly also trying to say that pollution, a key tool of the caste system to separate or deny people access to spaces, work, knowledge and dignity, occurs not through the nature of work done, but ‘from within’- from your thoughts, values and beliefs.

The text explains that therefore, Dalits can ‘invoke’ or ‘draw on’ a Fundamental Right (or Rights) in situations where they feel that they have been treated badly by some individual or community, or even by the government.

The text explains that they have drawn the attention of the government of India to the Constitution, demanding that the government abide by it and do justice to them.

The text explains that likewise, other minority groups have drawn on the Fundamental Rights section of our Constitution.

The text explains that they have particularly drawn upon the right to freedom of religion and cultural and educational rights.

The text explains that in the case of cultural and educational rights, distinct cultural and religious groups like the Muslims and Parsis have the right to be the guardians of the content of their culture, as well as the right to make decisions on how best this content is to be preserved.

The text explains that thus, by granting different forms of cultural rights, the Constitution tries to ensure cultural justice to such groups.

The text explains that the Constitution does this so that the culture of these groups is not dominated nor wiped out by the culture of the majority community.

The text explains that laws for the Marginalised As you have read, the government makes laws to protect its citizens.

The text explains that the government then makes an effort to promote such policies in order to give opportunities to specific groups.

The text explains that promoting Social Justice As part of their effort to implement the Constitution, both state and central governments create specific schemes for implementation in tribal areas or in areas that have a high Dalit population.

The text explains that for example, the government provides for free or subsidised hostels for students of Dalit and Adivasi communities so that they can avail of education facilities that may not be available in their localities.

The text explains that 77 Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation In addition to providing certain facilities, the government also operates through laws to ensure that concrete steps are taken to end inequity in the system.

The text explains that the laws which reserve seats in education and government employment for Dalits and Adivasis are based on an important argument- that in a society like ours, where for centuries sections of the population have been denied opportunities to learn and to work in order to develop new skills or vocations, a democratic government needs to step in and assist these sections.

The text explains that governments across India have their own list of Scheduled Castes (or Dalits), Scheduled Tribes and backward and most backward castes.

The text explains that students applying to educational institutions and those applying for posts in government are expected to furnish proof of their caste or tribe status, in the form of caste and tribe certificates.

The text explains that (Many government and educational institutions also ask for candidates to mention their caste/tribe status.) If a particular Dalit caste or a certain tribe is on the government list, then a candidate from that caste or tribe can avail of the benefit of reservation.

The text explains that for admission to colleges, especially to institutes of professional education, such as medical colleges, governments define a set of ‘cut-off’ marks.

The text explains that re-read the story on Rathnam as well as the provisions of the 1989 Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act.

The text explains that now list one reason why you think he used this law to file a complaint.

The text explains that janu, believe that Adivasis can also use this 1989 Act to fight against dispossession?

The text explains that is there anything specific in the provisions of the Act that allows her to believe this?

The text explains that the poems and the song in this Unit allow you to see the range of ways in which individuals and communities express their opinions, their anger and their sorrow.

The text explains that in class, do the following two exercises: (a) Bring to class a poem that discusses a social issue.

The text explains that work in small groups with two or more poems to discuss their meaning as well as what the poet is trying to communicate.

The text explains that (b)Identify a marginalised community in your locality.

The text explains that write a poem, or song, or draw a poster etc to express your feelings as a member of this community.

The text explains that gLOSSARY Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation 2024-25

Source: NCERT Class 8 Resources and Development, Chapter 6: Human Resources

prelims_traps: Do not treat 77 Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation and GLOSSARY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Human Resources; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Human Resources through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 77 Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation and GLOSSARY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
